full_response:
Let  
 R = { red particles }  G = { green particles }  B = { blue particles }  

h(p) ∈ [-100 n , 100 n].  
We must decide whether some r∈R,g∈G,b∈B satisfy

                             h(r)+2h(g)+4h(b)=8                        (1)

A direct enumeration of |R||G||B| triples is Θ(n³); even |R||G| or |G||B| is Θ(n²).  
The idea below reduces the problem to two polynomial convolutions which can be carried
out with the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) in Θ(n log n) time.

────────────────────────────────────
1 Encoding the three colour classes  
────────────────────────────────────
Let Δ = 100 n.  
The largest magnitude we will ever see after the coefficients (1,2,4) are applied is
4Δ = 400 n, so we choose an offset

        OFF  :=  400 n                       (2)

to make every exponent non–negative.

Create three Boolean arrays

        R[0 … 8·n]       G[0 … 8·n]       B[0 … 8·n]

(the exact upper bound will be fixed in §3).  
For every particle p do

 if c(p)=R             R[OFF +                h(p) ] ← 1  
 if c(p)=G             G[OFF + 2·h(p)        ] ← 1  
 if c(p)=B             B[OFF + 4·h(p)        ] ← 1

(The arrays contain 1 at an index iff at least one particle of the
corresponding colour contributes that exponent; multiplicity is irrelevant—we only
care whether a coefficient is 0 or ≥1.)

────────────────────────────────────
2 The polynomial viewpoint  
────────────────────────────────────
Treat the arrays as coefficients of polynomials

        P_R(x)= Σ R[i] xⁱ,   P_G(x)= Σ G[i] xⁱ,   P_B(x)= Σ B[i] xⁱ.

Because of (2) an r-term contributes OFF+h(r) to the exponent, a g-term
OFF+2h(g), and a b-term OFF+4h(b).  Hence the product

        P_R · P_G · P_B                                      (3)

contains the monomial x^{3·OFF+8}  iff there exist
indices i,j,k coming respectively from R,G,B such that

       (i-OFF) + 2(j-OFF) + 4(k-OFF) = 8,                   (4)

which is exactly equation (1).  
Thus:

Lemma  There is a Cooper triple  ⇔  the coefficient of x^{3·OFF+8}
in (3) is non–zero.

────────────────────────────────────
3 Computing the critical coefficient in Θ(n log n) time  
────────────────────────────────────
Convolutions of integer sequences can be obtained with two FFTs:

        (A * B) = FFT⁻¹( FFT(A) ⊙ FFT(B) ),

where ⊙ is point-wise multiplication.  
We proceed in two stages.

   a.    First convolution  
         S := R * G              //   Θ(m log m) with m = O(n)

   b.    Second convolution  
         T := S * B              //   another Θ(m log m)

We only need to guarantee that the arrays are long enough so that no
convolution “wraps around”.  The largest exponent that can appear is

       (OFF+Δ) + (OFF+2Δ) + (OFF+4Δ) = 3·OFF + 7Δ ≤ 3·(400n) + 700n < 2000n.

Hence choosing the array length to be the smallest power of two
M ≥ 2001 n is sufficient; M = Θ(n).

Pseudocode
────────────────
Cooper-Triple(S)
    Δ ← 100·n, OFF ← 4Δ                        // = 400n
    M ← 1; while M < 2001·n : M ← 2M           // next power of two
    allocate arrays R,G,B of length M filled with 0
    for each particle p in S
         case c(p)
             R: R[OFF + h(p)]     ← 1
             G: G[OFF + 2·h(p)]   ← 1
             B: B[OFF + 4·h(p)]   ← 1
    S ← IFFT( FFT(R) ⊙ FFT(G) )   // first convolution
    T ← IFFT( FFT(S) ⊙ FFT(B) )   // second convolution
    if round( T[3·OFF + 8] ) > 0
         return TRUE              // Cooper triple exists
    else
         return FALSE
────────────────

(The round(·) removes tiny numerical errors; an integer Number-Theoretic
Transform can be used instead to avoid floating point altogether.)

────────────────────────────────────
4 Correctness proof  
────────────────────────────────────
From Lemma 1 we only have to show that the algorithm returns
TRUE  iff  the x^{3·OFF+8} coefficient of P_R·P_G·P_B is positive.

Stage a computes the coefficient sequence of P_R·P_G; stage b multiplies
that sequence by P_B, yielding exactly the coefficient sequence of (3).
Therefore entry T[3·OFF+8] equals that coefficient.  The final “if” tests
whether it is non-zero, so the algorithm’s output obeys Lemma 1.
∎

────────────────────────────────────
5 Complexity analysis  
────────────────────────────────────
Array length M = Θ(n).  
Each FFT or inverse FFT costs Θ(M log M)=Θ(n log n).

• Two forward FFTs and two inverse FFTs ⇒ Θ(n log n) total time.  
• Three length-M arrays plus temporary FFT buffers ⇒ Θ(n) space.

Hence the required O(n log n) bound is met.

────────────────────────────────────
\boxed{\text{A Cooper triple can be detected in }O(n\log n)\text{ time and }O(n)\text{ space by encoding charges as exponents, performing two FFT–based convolutions, and seeing whether the coefficient of }x^{3\cdot 400n + 8}\text{ is non–zero.}}
extracted_answer: \text{A Cooper triple can be detected in }O(n\log n)\text{ time and }O(n)\text{ space by encoding charges as exponents, performing two FFT–based convolutions, and seeing whether the coefficient of }x^{3\cdot 400n + 8}\text{ is non–zero.}
